content.full_text:
STATE OF CALIFORNIA
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION
428 J Street • Suite 620 • Sacramento, CA 95814-2329
(916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886

April 10, 2017

Abdulmalik Abdulrahman-Wells
Senior Systems Engineer
Enterprise Networking Solutions, Inc.
3054 Fite Circle, Suite 106
Sacramento, CA 95827-1809

Re: Your Request for Advice
Our File No. A-17-055

Dear Mr. Abdulrahman-Wells:

This letter responds to your request for advice regarding post-government employment provisions of the Political Reform Act (the "Act") and Government Code Section 1090. ¹

Please note that we offer no opinion on the application of laws other than the Political Reform Act, such as the post-employment provisions of Public Contract Code Section 10411. Additionally, this letter is based on the facts presented. The Fair Political Practices Commission does not act as a finder of fact when it provides advice. (In re Oglesby (1975) 1 FPPC Ops. 71.)

Regarding our advice on Section 1090, we are required to forward your request and all pertinent facts relating to the request to the Attorney General's Office and the Sacramento County District Attorney's Office, which we have done. (Section 1097.1(c)(3).) We will notify you if we receive a written response from either entity. (Section 1097.1(c)(4).) Additionally, for purposes of Section 1090 the following advice "is not admissible in a criminal proceeding against any individual other than the requestor." (See Section 1097.1(c)(5).)

QUESTIONS

May you work on the Department of Technology's ("CDT") Storage as a Service ("SaaS") contract; or its Invitation for Bid ("IFB") for Network Services and any resulting contract as an employee of a private company, where you were employed by CDT within the past twelve months?

¹ The Political Reform Act is contained in Government Code Sections 81000 through 91014. All statutory references are to the Government Code, unless otherwise indicated. The regulations of the Fair Political Practices Commission are contained in Sections 18110 through 18997 of Title 2 of the California Code of Regulations. All regulatory references are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations, unless otherwise indicated.

File No. A-17-055
Page No. 2

CONCLUSIONS

The "one-year ban" is applicable to you and prohibits you as an employee of a private
company from communicating or appearing before CDT to influence the issuance, amendment,
awarding, or revocation of a contract with CDT, or the sale or purchase of goods or property to
CDT for the period of one year from your date of permanent separation from CDT. In regards to the
two contracts at issue:

1. SaaS contract: You may administer, implement, or fulfill the requirements of this existing
contract during the one-year ban. However, you may not engage in communications or appearances
to influence the amendment or revocation of the contract, or the sale or purchase of goods or
property.

2. Network Services IFB; Resulting contract: You may not engage in communications or
any appearances to influence CDT related to the IFB process during the one-year ban. However,
you may advise and draft proposals for ENS, to the extent that you are not identified with ENS'
efforts to bid on the project. If ENS is awarded the contract, you may work to implement or
administer the contract, so long as you do not engage in communications or appearances to
influence changes to the contract or any purchase/sale agreements during the ban.

FACTS

You were previously employed as a "Systems Software Specialist III (Tech)" with the
Department of Technology ("CDT")² until you permanently left state service in November 2016. In
your duties with CDT you worked as a network and security engineer, and specialized in
virtualization, cloud computing, wireless, and virtual private networks. Your duties included
evaluating and testing software and hardware ("bake-offs"), which would then lead to purchasing
decisions by your supervisors. You state you never interacted at the level of contracting,
negotiating, or purchasing decisions at CDT regarding any provider of services.

You currently are employed by Enterprise Networking Solutions, Inc. ("ENS"), a
professional technical consulting firm serving state and local government agencies as well as
private sector companies, where you have a similar set of duties. ENS is a Value-Added Reseller
for many products.

While you were employed with CDT, the agency prepared an Invitation for Bids ("IFB") for
cloud-based or "Amazon-type" storage. Your participation in forming this bid was the preparation
of three "generic" network-related questions to assess a bidder's network capabilities. No one
responded to this IFB. CDT formulated a new storage-related IFB, this time for a Storage as a
Service ("SaaS") contract, where the vendor installs storage equipment at CDT and manages the
equipment. This second IFB was issued after you left CDT, and awarded to ENS after you began
your employment there. This contract did not involve your duties at ENS until a recent network
issue arose, and your expertise was sought.

² CDT is the state's central IT organization and has broad responsibility and authority over all aspects of
technology in state government, including: policy formation, interagency coordination, IT project oversight,
information security, technology service delivery, and advocacy.

File No. A-17-055
Page No. 3

CDT is in the process of preparing another IFB to contract out its networking services to a
provider that can work on several different systems. Currently, CDT is reliant on a number of
contract engineers who work only on specific network services (for example: a Cisco engineer
works only on Cisco, and a F5 engineer works only on F5). You state that you were asked your
opinion on this proposal in a staff meeting while employed at CDT. You responded that you
thought this was a "good idea" and you noted a company unrelated to ENS that did this type of
work. You were asked what type of network service help you needed for your projects, and you
responded that "you did not need any." You describe your comments and participation as a "casual
recommendation" as opposed to a formal or written recommendation. Your current employer, ENS
wishes to bid on this IBF, and fulfilling the requirements of this contract would be part of your
duties.

ANALYSIS

Section 1090

Section 1090 generally prohibits public officers, while acting in their official capacities,
from making contracts in which they are financially interested. Section 1090 is concerned with
financial interests, other than remote or minimal interests, that prevent public officials from
exercising absolute loyalty and undivided allegiance in furthering the best interests of their
agencies. (Stigall v. City of Taft (1962) 58 Cal.2d 565, 569.) Section 1090 is intended "not only to
strike at actual impropriety, but also to strike at the appearance of impropriety." (City of Imperial
Beach v. Bailey (1980) 103 Cal.App.3d 191, 197.) Under Section 1090, "the prohibited act is the
making of a contract in which the official has a financial interest." (People v. Honig (1996) 48
Cal.App.4th 289, 333.) A contract that violates Section 1090 is void. (Thomson v. Call (1985) 38
Cal.3d 633, 646.) The prohibition applies regardless of whether the terms of the contract are fair
and equitable to all parties. (Id. at pp. 646-649.)

Leaving state employment may not avoid a Section 1090 violation when the person has been
involved in the contract process. In City Council v. McKinley (1978) 80 Cal. App.3d 204, 212, the
court stated:

If the date of final execution were the only time at which a conflict might occur, a
city councilman could do all the work negotiating and affecting a final contract
which would be available only to himself and then present the matter to the
council, resigning his office immediately before the contract was executed. He
would reap the benefits of his work without being on the council when the final
act was completed. This is not the spirit or the intent of the law which precludes
an officer from involving himself in the making of a contract.

Similarly, the Attorney General's Office has opined that county employees could not
propose an agreement for consulting services, then resign, and provide the proposed services (66
Ops.Cal.Atty.Gen. 156 (1983)) and a council member could not participate in the establishment of a
loan program and then leave office and apply for a loan (81 Ops.Cal.Atty.Gen. 317 (1998).)

File No. A-17-055
Page No. 4

Section 1090 reaches beyond the officials who execute the contract, and courts have broadly
interpreted "the making of a contract" when applying the section:

"The decisional law, therefore, has not interpreted section 1090 in a
hypertechnical manner, but holds that an official (or a public employee) may be
convicted of violation no matter whether he actually participated personally in the
execution of the questioned contract, if it is established that he had the
opportunity to, and did, influence execution directly or indirectly promote his
personal interests."

(People v. Sobel (1974) 40 Cal.App.3d 1046, 1052.)

Thus, participation in the "making of a contract" is defined broadly and includes "the
planning, preliminary discussions, compromises, drawing of plans and specifications and
solicitation of bids . . .." (Stigall v. City of Taft, supra, at pp. 569-571; see also Millbrae Assn. for
Residential Survival v. City of Millbrae (1968) 262 Cal.App.2d 222, 237.)

SaaS contract: Under the facts provided, you were not involved in the planning,
negotiations, or solicitation for bids related to this contract and you are not prohibited under Section
1090 from working on this contract.

Networking Services IFB: CDT is currently preparing the IFB for Network Services. Your
participation while employed at CDT involved your comments at one staff meeting that: you
thought the approach was "a good idea," your offer of a company that you were aware provided this
type of service, and that you did not need this type of service for your current projects. You
were not involved on a more detailed level in forming the specifications or requirements of the IFB;
which we note is still in the information process. Your input did not relate to requirements for the
IFB, or specific details of the project. You were not asked to evaluate or "test" potential bidders on
the contract. Your input was what you describe as a "casual recommendation" in response to a
general question posed to staff. Looking at the level of involvement, it does not appear from these
facts that your comments had an "influence," on the formation of the IFB, other than that of a
general assent. Therefore, you are not prohibited under Section 1090 from working on this contract.

Revolving Door Prohibitions

Public officials who leave state service are subject to two types of post-governmental
employment provisions under the Act: the "one-year ban" and the "permanent ban." These
provisions are commonly referred to as the "revolving door" prohibitions; and require a separate
analysis from that of Section 1090.

Permanent Ban

The "permanent ban" prohibits a former state employee from "switching sides" and
participating, for compensation, in certain proceedings involving the State of California and other
specific parties, or assisting in the proceeding if the proceeding is one in which the former state
employee participated while employed by the state. (Sections 87401 and 87402; Regulation
17441.1.)

File No. A-17-055
Page No. 5

The permanent ban is a lifetime ban and applies to any judicial, quasi-judicial, or other
proceeding in which the employee participated while serving as a state administrative official.
(Section 87400(b).) "Judicial, quasi-judicial or other proceeding" includes contract proceedings.
(Section 87400(c).) An official or employee has "participated" in a proceeding if he or she took part
in the proceeding "personally, and substantially" through "decision, approval, disapproval, formal
written recommendation, rendering advice on a substantial basis, investigation, or use of
confidential information . . . ." (Section 87400(d).)

The permanent ban does not apply to "new" proceedings or separate proceedings. New
contracts with the employee's former agency in which the former employee did not participate are
considered new proceedings, even when the parties are the same. (Leslie Advice Letter, No. I-89-
649.) The application, drafting, and awarding of a contract, license, or approval is considered a
proceeding separate from the monitoring and performance of the contract, license, or approval.
(Starovgylov Advice Letter, No. A-14-149, citing Anderson, supra, and Blonien Advice Letter, No.
A-89-463.)

SaaS contract: Under the facts you have provided, the permanent ban does not prevent you
from performing duties related to the implementation of the SaaS contract. This contract arose out
of a IFB in which you did not participate. Furthermore, your duties at ENS would involve you in
the implementation of the SaaS contract, which is a "new" proceeding separate from the formation
and awarding of the contract.

Network Services IFB: CDT is currently preparing the IFB for Network Services. Your
participation while employed at CDT involved your input at one staff meeting. According to your
facts, your input was on a general level and these services did not relate to any of your work
projects at the time. You were not further involved on a more detailed level in forming the
specifications or requirements of the IFB. Therefore, your involvement does not rise to the level of
participation required under Section 87400(d), in that it was not of "a personal and substantial"
nature through the "rendering of advice on a substantial basis." The permanent ban does not apply
to the Network Services IFB or a resulting contract.

One-Year Ban

The second restriction is the "one-year ban" which prohibits a former state employee from
making, for compensation, any formal or informal appearance, or making any oral or written
communication, before his or her former agency for the purpose of influencing any administrative
or legislative actions or any discretionary act involving the "issuance,ishment, awarding, or
revocation of a . . . contract, or the sale or purchase of goods or property." (See Section 87406;
Regulation 18746.1.) Unlike the permanent ban, this ban is not confined to particular proceedings.

The one-year ban applies to any employee of a state administrative agency who holds a
position that is designated or should be designated in the agency's conflict-of-interest code. (Section
87406(d)(1); Regulation 18746.1(a)(4).) An employee should be designated when that employee
holds a position that entails making or participating in making decisions. The ban applies for twelve
months from the date the employee permanently leaves state office or employment. While in effect,
the one-year ban applies only when a former employee or official is being compensated for his or
her appearance or communication.

File No. A-17-055
Page No. 6

her appearances or communications before his or her former agency on behalf of any person as an
agent, attorney, or representative of that person. (Regulation 18746.1(b)(3) and (4).)

An appearance or communication is for the "purpose of influencing" if it is made for the
"principal purpose of supporting, promoting, influencing, modifying, opposing, delaying, or
advancing the action or proceeding." (Regulation 18746.2.) An appearance or communication
includes, but is not limited to, conversing by telephone or in person, corresponding in writing or by
electronic transmission, attending a meeting, and delivering or sending any communication. (Id.)

Finally, as relevant to these facts, appearances and communications are prohibited if they
are (1) before a state agency that the public official worked for or represented, or (2) before a state
agency "which budget, personnel, and other operations" are subject to the control of a state agency
the public official worked for or represented. (Regulation 18746.1(b)(6).)

We point out, however, that not all communications are prohibited by the one-year ban. For
example, so long as not otherwise prohibited by under the permanent ban, services performed to
administer, implement, or fulfill the requirements of an existing contract, or sale agreement are
excluded from the one-year ban, provided the services do not involve the issuance, amendment,
awarding, or revocation of any of these actions or proceedings. (Regulation 18746.1(c); Williams
Advice Letter No. I-06-058 citing to Duiring Advice Letter, No. A-03-272 and Hanan Advice
Letter, No. I-00-209.) Additionally, a former agency official may draft proposals on a client's behalf
to be submitted to the agency. (Cook Advice Letter, No. A-95-321; Harrison Advice Letter, No. A-
92-289); or may use his or her expertise to advise clients on the procedural requirements, plans, or
policies of his or her former agency (Perry Advice Letter, No. A-94-004), so long as the former
employee is not identified in the client's efforts to influence the agency. ³

Because your duties at CDT included making evaluations that your supervisors relied upon
in making their purchasing decisions, you participated in the making of decisions. (Robards Advice
Letter, No. I-10-116, citing Unterreiner Advice Letter, No. I-98-299.) Your position was or should
have been a designated position. (Section 87302.⁴) As a consultant for ENS, you are working for
compensation and in representation of ENS. Accordingly, the one-year ban applies to you.

SaaS contract

In regards to the SaaS contract, to the extent that your position with ENS requires you to
"administer, implement, or fulfill the requirements of an existing contract" you may work to
implement the contract. (Regulation 18746.1(c).) However, under the one-year ban you may not
engage in communications or appearances to influence, as relevant here, the amendment, award, or

³ Regulation 18746.2 (1)(b) provides some additional exceptions, as follows:
"An appearance or communication is not limited by this section when an individual: Participates as a panelist
or formal speaker at a conference or similar public event for educational purposes or to disseminate research
and the subject matter does not pertain to a specific action or proceeding; (2) Attends a general informational
meeting, seminar, or similar event; (3) Requests information concerning any matter of public record; or (4)
Communicates with the press."

⁴ Section 87302 requires agencies to enumerate positions which involve the making or participating in the
making of decisions which may foreseeably have a material effect on any financial interest.

File No. A-17-055
Page No. 7

revocation of a contract, or the sale or purchase of goods or property. (Section 87406(d)(1);
Regulation 18746.1(b).)

Network Services IFB; Resulting contract

The one-year ban prohibits appearances and communications made for the "purpose of
influencing" a discretionary act, such as the award of a contract. Therefore, you may not appear or
communicate with CDT in the IFB process on behalf of ENS. As noted above, an appearance or
communication includes conversing by telephone or in person, corresponding in writing or by
electronic transmission, attending a meeting, and delivering or sending any communication.
You may advise ENS on CDT requirements, plans or policy, and you may draft proposals on ENS'
behalf, but only to the extent that you are not identified in any of these actions. If ENS is awarded
the contract, you may work to implement or administer the contract, so long as you do not engage
in communications or appearances to influence changes to the contract or any purchase/sale
agreements during the one-year period.

If you have other questions on this matter, please contact me at (916) 322-5660.

Sincerely,

Hyla P. Wagner
General Counsel

By: L. Karen Harrison
Counsel, Legal Division

LKH:jgl

from making contracts in which they are financially interested. Section l 090 is concerned with

"The decisional law, therefore, has not interpreted section 1090 in a 
hypertechnical manner, but holds that an official (or a public employee) may be

solicitation of bids . . .. " (Stigall v. City of Taft, supra, at pp. 569-571; see also Millbrae Assn. for 
Residential Sunival v. City of Millbrae (1968) 262 Cal.App.2d 222, 23 7.) 
Saas contract: Under the facts provided, you were not involved in the planning, 
negotiations, or solicitation for bids related to this contract and you are not prohibited under Section 
1090 from working on this contract. 
Networking Services IFB: CDT is currently preparing the IFB for Network Services. Your 
participation while employed at CDT involved your comments at one staff meeting that: you

facts that your comments had an '•influence," on the formation of the lFB, other than that of a

Revolving Door Prohibitions 
Public officials who leave state service are subject to two types of post-governmental 
employment provisions under the Act: the '"one-year ban" and the "permanent ban." These 
provisions are commonly referred to as the "revolving door" prohibitions; and require a separate 
analysis from that of Section 1090. 
Permanent Ban 
The "permanent ban" prohibits a former state employee from "switching sides" and 
participating, for compensation, in certain proceedings involving the State of California and other 
specific parties, or assisting in the proceeding if the proceeding is one in which the former state 
employee participated while employed by the state. (Sections 87401 and 87402; Regulation 
18741.1.)  
The pennanent ban is a lifetime ban and applies to any judicial, quasi-judicial, or other 
proceeding in which the employee participated while serving as a state administrative official. 
(Section 87400(b).) "Judicial, quasi-judicial or other proceeding" includes contract proceedings. 
(Section 87400(c).) An official or employee has "participated" in a proceeding ifhe or she took part 
in the proceeding "personally, and substantially" through "decision, approval, disapproval, fonnal 
written recommendation, rendering advice on a substantial basis, investigation, or use of 
confidential infonnation . . .. '' (Section 87400(d).) 
The pennanent ban does not apply to "new" proceedings or separate proceedings. New 
contracts with the employee's fonner agency in which the fonner employee did not participate are 
considered new proceedings, even when the parties are the same. (Leslie Advice Letter, No. 1-89-
649.) The application, drafting, and awarding of a contract, license, or approval is considered a 
proceeding separate from the monitoring and perfonnance of the contract, license, or approval. 
(Starovoytov Advice Letter, No. A-14-149, citing Anderson, supra, and Blonien Advice Letter, No. 
A-89-463.) 
SaaS contract: Under the facts you have provided, the pennanent ban does not prevent you 
from perfonning duties related to the implementation of the Saas contract. This contract arose out 
of a IFB in which you did not participate. Furthennore, your duties at ENS would involve you in 
the implementation of the SaaS contract, which is a "new" proceeding separate from the fonnation 
and awarding of the contract. 
Network Services IFB: CDT is currently preparing the IFB for Network Services. Your 
participation while employed at CDT involved your input at one staff meeting. According to your 
facts, your input was on a general level and these services did not relate to any of your work 
projects at the time. You were not further involved on a more detailed level in forming the 
specifications or requirements of the IFB. Therefore, your involvement does not rise to the level of 
participation required under Section 87400(d), in that it was not of "a personal and substantial" 
nature through the "rendering of advice on a substantial basis." The permanent ban does not apply 
to the Network Services IFB or a resulting contract. 
One-Year Ban 
The second restriction is the "one-year ban" which prohibits a former state employee from 
making, for compensation, any formal or infonnal appearance, or making any oral or written 
communication, before his or her former agency for the purpose of influencing any administrative 
or legislative actions or any discretionary act involving the "issuance, amendment, awarding, or 
revocation of a . . .  contract, or the sale or purchase of goods or property." (See Section 87406; 
Regulation 18746.1.) Unlike the permanent ban, this ban is not confined to particular proceedings. 
The one-year ban applies to any employee of a state administrative agency who holds a 
position that is designated or should be designated in the agency's conflict-of-interest code. (Section 
87406(d)(l ); Regulation 18746.l(a)(4).) An employee should be designated when that employee 
holds a position that entails making or participating in making decisions. The ban applies for twelve 
months from the date the employee permanently leaves state office or employment. While in effect, 
the one-year ban applies only when a fonner employee or official is being compensated for his or  
her appearances or communications before his or her former agency on behalf of any person as an 
agent, attorney, or representative of that person. (Regulation 18746.l(b)(3) and (4).) 
An appearance or communication is for the "purpose of influencing" if it is made for the 
"principal purpose of supporting, promoting, influencing, modifying, opposing, delaying, or 
advancing the action or proceeding." (Regulation 18746.2.) An appearance or communication 
includes, but is not limited to, conversing by telephone or in person, corresponding in writing or by 
electronic transmission, attending a meeting, and delivering or sending any communication. (Id.) 
Finally, as relevant to these facts, appearances and communications are prohibited if they 
are (I) before a state agency that the public official worked for or represented, or (2) before a state 
agency "which budget, personnel, and other operations" are subject to the control of a state agency 
the public official worked for or represented. (Regulation 18746.l(b)(6).) 
We point out, however, that not all communications are prohibited by the one-year ban. For 
example, so long as not otherwise prohibited by under the permanent ban, services performed to 
administer, implement, or fulfill the requirements of an existing contract, or sale agreement are 
excluded from the one-year ban, provided the services do not involve the issuance, amendment, 
awarding, or revocation of any of these actions or proceedings. (Regulation I 8746.1 ( c ); Williams 
Advice Letter No. 1-06-058 citing to Quiring Advice Letter, No. A-03-272 and Hanan Advice 
Letter, No. 1-00-209.) Additionally, a former agency official may draft proposals on a client1s behalf 
to be submitted to the agency. (Cook Advice Letter, No. A-95-321; Harrison Advice Letter, No. A-
92-289); or may use his or her expertise to advise clients on the procedural requirements, plans, or 
policies of his or her former agency (Perry Advice Letter, No. A-94-004), so long as the former 
employee is not identified in the client1s efforts to influence the agency. 3 
Because your duties at CDT included making evaluations that your supervisors relied upon 
in making their purchasing decisions, you participated in the making of decisions. (Robards Advice 
Letter, No.1-10-116, citing Umerreiner Advice Letter, No. 1-98-299.) Your position was or should 
have been a designated position. (Section 87302.4) As a consultant for ENS, you are working for 
compensation and in representation of ENS. Accordingly, the one-year ban applies to you. 
SaaS contract 
In regards to the Saas contract, to the extent that your position with ENS requires you to 
"administer, implement, or fulfill the requirements of an existing contract" you may work to 
implement the contract. (Regulation 18746.l(c).) However, under the one-year ban you may not 
engage in communications or appearances to influence, as relevant here, the amendment, award, or 
3 Regulation 18746.2 (l)(b) provides some additional exceptions, as follows: 
"An appearance or communication is not limited by this section when an individual: Participates as a panelist 
or fonnal speaker at a conference or similar public event for educational purposes or to disseminate research 
and the subject matter does not pertain to a specific action or proceeding; (2) Attends a general infonnational 
meeting, seminar, or similar event; (3) Requests infonnation concerning any matter of public record; or (4) 
Communicates with the press." 
4 Section 87302 requires agencies to enumerate positions which involve the making or participating in the 
making of decisions which may foreseeably have a material effect on any financial interest.  
revocation of a contract, or the sale or purchase of goods or property. (Section 87406(d)(l ); 
Regulation 18746.l(b).) 
Network Services IFB; Resulting contract 
The one-year ban prohibits appearances and communications made for the 11purpose of 
influencing" a discretionary act, such as the award of a contract. Therefore, you may not appear or 
communicate with CDT in the IFB process on behalf of ENS. As noted above, an appearance or 
communication includes conversing by telephone or in person, corresponding in writing or by 
electronic transmission, attending a meeting, and delivering or sending any communication. 
You may advise ENS on CDT requirements, plans or policy; and you may draft proposals on ENS' 
behalf, but only to the extent that you are not identified in any of these actions. If ENS is awarded 
the contract, you may work to implement or administer the contract, so long as you do not engage 
in communications or appearances to influence changes to the contract or any purchase/sale 
agreements during the one-year period.

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: May you work on the Department of Technology's ("CDT") Storage as a Service ("SaaS") 
contract; or its Invitation for Bid ("IFB") for Network Services and any resulting contract as an 
employee of a private company, where you were employed by CDT within the past twelve months? 
1 The Political Refonn Act is contained in

CONCLUSION: The "one-year ban" is applicable to you and prohibits you as an employee of a private 
company from communicating or appearing before CDT to influence the issuance, amendment, 
awarding, or revocation of a contract with CDT, or the sale or purchase of goods or property to 
CDT for the period of one year from your date of pennanent separation from CDT. In regards to the 
two contracts at issue: 
1. SaaS contract: You may administer, implement, or fulfill the requirements of this existing 
contract during the one-year ban. However, you may not engage in communications or appearances 
to influence the amendment or revocation of the contract, or the sale or purchase of goods or 
property. 
2. Network Sen1ices IFB; Resulting contract: You may not engage in communications or 
any appearances to influence CDT related to the IFB process during the one-year ban. However, 
you may advise and draft proposals for ENS, to the extent that you are not identified with ENS' 
efforts to bid on the project. If ENS is awarded the contract, you may work to implement or 
administer the contract, so long as you do not engage in communications or appearances to 
influence changes to the contract or any purchase/sale agreements during the ban.

Additionally, this letter is based on the facts presented. The Fair Political Practices Commission does not act as a finder of fact when it provides advice. (In re Oglesby (1975) 1 FPPC Ops. 71.) Regarding our advice on Section 1090, we are required to forward your request and all pertinent facts relating to the request to the Attorney General's Office and the Sacramento County District Attorney's Office, which we have done. (Section 1097.1(c)(3).) We will notify you if we receive a written response from either entity. (Section 1097.1(c)(4).) Additionally, for purposes of Section 1090 the following advice "is not admissible in a criminal proceeding against any individual other than the requestor." (See Section 1097.1(c)(5).) All statutory references are to the Government Code, unless otherwise indicated. The regulations of the Fair Political Practices Commission are contained in Sections 18110 through 18997 of Title 2 of the California Code of Regulations. All regulatory references are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations, unless otherwise indicated.
